Patch Set 4:

would be nice if we get another round of reviews here?

James, I think Neil patch did not apply here (not that I remember what was that patch, but I don't remember anythign applicable anyway)

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

I don't understand his code.
If the code never sleeps while the lock is held, then a mutex cannot help.  If the code does sleep, then a spinlock is broken.

Why do you think that a mutex will help at all?

Patch Set 4:

> I don't understand his code.
 > If the code never sleeps while the lock is held, then a mutex
 > cannot help.  If the code does sleep, then a spinlock is broken.
 > 
 > Why do you think that a mutex will help at all?

it's not the code that holds the spinlock. The concern is the spinlock vs mutes implementation itself.

In face of heavy contention spinlocks spin wasting cpu cycles that could be better spent doing something else. when a contended mutex is encountered the cpu is yielded to other threads that could do something useful with it.

Patch Set 4: -Code-Review

> In face of heavy contention spinlocks spin wasting cpu cycles that could be better spent doing something else.

Yes of course - in the fresh morning light that is now obvious :-)

Though mutexes are optimized to spin opportunistically - if the thread holding the mutex has running on a CPU, threads waiting for the mutex will still spin for a while - but not as long if the mutex is held for a long time.

It would be better to reduce the time that the lock is held for.  Scheduling might be better than spinning, but it is not exactly cheap.

Are there any identified hot-spots that we could look at optimizing?

A quick looks shows wel over 100 locked regions, so analyising them all is a bit tedious.  Quite a few seem to be just protecting flag bits.  Others are protecting lookups or modification is hashtables or interval trees.  Maybe want to keep the spin lock for the flags and have a read/write lock (or RCU) for the data structure modifications.

I don't see any actual problems with the change, but it seems like the wrong way to fix the problem.  I'd rather take the interval tree and hash table out from under the lock, and then see what is left.

Patch Set 4:

I agree with Neil here. Take a look at https://review.whamcloud.com/#/c/35483 which removes l_lock. Their is also https://review.whamcloud.com/#/c/36218 which should reduce the contention for the ldlm hash table. I think we can take similar approaches with the other locks.

parents.subject: LU-7912 osc: Remove stale comment in osc_page_transfer_add

commit.subject: LU-4801 ldlm: use mutex instead of spinlock on server side resources
commit.message:
LU-4801 ldlm: use mutex instead of spinlock on server side resources

Since server side processing of resources during granting is potentially
very cpu-intensive, it well might be that spinlock is
improper choice and by using mutex we allow other threads to do something
useful instead.

Change-Id: I05129f07d32914ce68c30416f71ab92a4c581c53
Signed-off-by Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
